title: Anyone can burn arbitrary users' tokens via the inverted allowance check in burnFrom
claim: `burnFrom` validates and decrements `_allowances[msg.sender][from]` instead of `_allowances[from][msg.sender]`. An attacker can therefore create the required allowance entry themselves by calling `approve(victim, amount)` from their own account, then call `burnFrom(victim, amount)` to destroy the victim's balance.
impact: Any unprivileged attacker can permanently destroy tokens from arbitrary holders without consent, causing direct loss of funds and permissionless denial of service against users, treasuries, exchanges, liquidity pools, or other integrations that hold the token.
paths: Attacker calls `approve(victim, amount)` from their own address, which sets `_allowances[attacker][victim] = amount`.
The contract accepts the attacker-controlled allowance entry, reduces it, and burns `victim`'s tokens.

title: Upgrade leaves the hidden legacy ledger live through non-deprecated write paths
claim: Once `upgrade()` sets `deprecated = true`, canonical ERC20 writes and views (`approve`, `transfer`, `transferFrom`, `balanceOf`, `allowance`, `totalSupply`) redirect to `upgradedAddress`, but several other state-mutating functions still operate directly on the legacy `_balances`, `_allowances`, and `_totalSupply` mappings. In particular, `burn`, `burnFrom`, both `bulkTransfer` overloads, both `bulkTransferFrom` overloads, `mint`, and `permit` keep mutating the old ledger even though users and integrators are shown only the upgraded contract's balances and allowances.
impact: The token can enter a split-brain state where a hidden legacy ledger remains live after migration. If the upgraded token mirrors balances or supply from the old token, holders and spenders can effectively double-spend or create mismatched supply/accounting across the two ledgers. Even when balances are not mirrored, users and integrations can still suffer invisible transfers, hidden approvals, covert burns, and severe accounting inconsistencies because legacy balances remain usable through non-standard helper functions while standard ERC20 views point elsewhere.
paths: Owner calls `upgrade(newToken)`, which flips `deprecated` and redirects standard ERC20 functions and views to `newToken`.
A holder with legacy balance calls `bulkTransfer(...)` and moves old-ledger tokens even though `transfer()` is deprecated and `balanceOf()` no longer reports that old balance.

A spender uses a legacy approval created before upgrade, or a hidden allowance created via `permit()` after upgrade, then spends it through `bulkTransferFrom(...)` even though `allowance()` and `transferFrom()` now reference only the upgraded contract.
Additional legacy write paths such as `burn`, `burnFrom`, and `mint` continue mutating the hidden old ledger, worsening supply and accounting divergence.
